content:
# Matthew Chapter 25 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Matthew Chapter 25 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 25:1 — "virgins" vs. "bridesmaids" for παρθένοι

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "ten virgins who, having taken the lamps of them" | Literal — παρθένοι as "virgins" |
| LOGOS | "ten virgins who took their lamps" | Formal — preserves "virgins" |
| PNEUMA | "ten bridesmaids who took their lamps" | Dynamic — replaces "virgins" with the social role |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "virgins" (EMET/LOGOS). The Greek παρθένοι specifically means "virgins" — unmarried young women. PNEUMA's "bridesmaids" conveys the social function but drops the word that gives the parable its traditional name and its specific cultural meaning. In a first-century Jewish wedding, the virginity of the attendants was part of the social fabric, not incidental.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. "Ten Virgins" is the parable's recognized title.

---

## 25:1 — "lamps" vs. "lamps" (λαμπάδας)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the lamps of them" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "their lamps" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "their lamps" | Dynamic |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three agree on "lamps." No divergence on the noun itself. λαμπάδας refers to torches or oil lamps used in wedding processions.

**Status:** No significant divergence.

---

## 25:6 — "Behold, the bridegroom!" vs. "The groom is here!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Behold, the bridegroom! Come out to meet him" | Literal, preserving ἰδού and "bridegroom" |
| LOGOS | "Behold, the bridegroom! Come out to meet him" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "The groom is here! Come out to meet him!" | Dynamic — "groom" for "bridegroom," drops "behold" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Behold, the bridegroom!" (LOGOS). "Behold" (ἰδού) is a dramatic announcement marker — in this midnight context, it creates urgency. "Bridegroom" (νυμφίος) is the full form and carries christological weight in Matthew. PNEUMA's "groom" is truncated.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 25:13 — Sinaiticus shorter reading

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Watch therefore, for you do not know the day nor the hour" | Short reading, following Sinaiticus |
| LOGOS | "Watch therefore, for you know neither the day nor the hour" | Same short reading |
| PNEUMA | "So stay alert! You don't know the day or the hour" | Same short reading, dynamic phrasing |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three correctly follow the Sinaiticus reading, omitting "in which the Son of Man comes." SOPHIA adopted LOGOS phrasing. PNEUMA's "stay alert" for γρηγορεῖτε is a valid dynamic rendering, but "watch" is the more precise translation of this verb, which implies vigilant wakefulness.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Textual agreement across all three.

---

## 25:15 — "talents" vs. "bags of gold"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "five talents... two... one" | Transliterated unit of measurement |
| LOGOS | "five talents... two... one" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "five bags of gold... two bags... one bag" | Dynamic — renders the unit as a physical description |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "talents" (EMET/LOGOS). The word "talent" (τάλαντον) has entered the English language directly from this parable. It is universally recognized and needs no replacement. PNEUMA's "bags of gold" is inaccurate — a talent was a unit of weight (roughly 75 pounds) applied to silver or gold, not a "bag." The dynamic rendering loses precision and historical grounding.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. "Talents" is both historically accurate and culturally recognizable.

---

## 25:21 — "Well done" vs. "Excellent"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Well [done], good and faithful slave" | Literal with "slave" for δοῦλος |
| LOGOS | "Well done, good and faithful servant" | Formal — the iconic phrase |
| PNEUMA | "Excellent! You are a good and faithful servant" | Dynamic — restructured with "Excellent" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "Well done, good and faithful servant" (LOGOS). This is one of the most recognized phrases in the English Bible. PNEUMA's "Excellent!" loses the specific commendation formula. The phrase εὖ, δοῦλε ἀγαθὲ καὶ πιστέ deserves its traditional rendering.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Iconic biblical phrasing preserved.

---

## 25:29 — "For to everyone who has, more will be given" vs. dynamic paraphrase

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For to everyone having, it will be given, and he will have abundance" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "For to everyone who has, more will be given, and he will have an abundance" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "For those who use well what they are given will receive more, and they will have an abundance" | Dynamic — adds "use well" as interpretive gloss |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS rendering. PNEUMA's "use well" is an interpretation — the Greek simply says "to the one having" (τῷ ἔχοντι). The parable's context implies faithful use, but the saying itself is deliberately stark and paradoxical. The reader should feel the apparent unfairness before understanding the parable's logic.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's interpretive addition is not adopted.

---

## 25:30 — "worthless" vs. "useless" for ἀχρεῖον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the useless slave" | Literal — ἀχρεῖος as "useless" |
| LOGOS | "the worthless servant" | Formal — "worthless" carries moral weight |
| PNEUMA | "this useless servant" | Dynamic — "useless" same as EMET |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "worthless" (LOGOS). ἀχρεῖος means "useless, unprofitable." "Worthless" is slightly stronger and carries the moral judgment that the parable intends — this servant has failed to fulfill his purpose. "Useless" is more neutral.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor vocabulary preference.

---

## 25:34 — "from the foundation of the world" vs. "since the creation of the world"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "from [the] foundation of [the] world" | Literal rendering of ἀπὸ καταβολῆς κόσμου |
| LOGOS | "from the foundation of the world" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "since the creation of the world" | Dynamic — "creation" for "foundation" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "from the foundation of the world" (LOGOS). The Greek καταβολή means "laying down, foundation" — not "creation" (κτίσις). The image is architectural: God laid the foundation of the kingdom before the world began. PNEUMA's "creation" is close but misses the specific metaphor.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 25:40 — "the least of these my brothers" vs. "brothers and sisters"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "one of these the least of the brothers of me" | Ultra-literal with Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "one of the least of these my brothers" | Formal English |
| PNEUMA | "one of the least of these brothers and sisters of mine" | Dynamic — adds "and sisters" for inclusivity |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the least of these my brothers" (LOGOS). The Greek ἀδελφῶν is masculine plural, which in Greek can include mixed groups. However, SOPHIA follows the literal form here because the referent of "brothers" is itself debated — it may mean all suffering people, or specifically Jesus' disciples, or the Jewish people. Adding "and sisters" is a modern inclusive rendering that resolves an ambiguity the text intentionally preserves.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The Greek's own ambiguity is maintained.

---

## 25:46 — Symmetry of αἰώνιον

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "eternal punishment... eternal life" | Preserves the symmetry |
| LOGOS | "eternal punishment... eternal life" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "eternal punishment... eternal life" | Same |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three translators preserve the parallel structure with the same adjective (αἰώνιον) for both punishment and life. No divergence.

**Status:** Full agreement. No resolution needed.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA replacing traditional biblical terms | 3 | Traditional terms preserved (virgins, talents, well done) |
| PNEUMA interpretive additions | 2 | Greek preserved without expansion |
| Vocabulary precision (worthless/useless, foundation/creation) | 2 | More precise term adopted |
| Inclusive language expansion | 1 | Greek ambiguity preserved |
| Minor phrasing differences | 2 | LOGOS preferred |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 25 contains some of the most iconic language in the English Bible ("Well done, good and faithful servant," the parable of the talents, the sheep and the goats). PNEUMA's dynamic renderings ("bridesmaids," "bags of gold," "Excellent!") risk disrupting the deep familiarity and cultural resonance of these passages. SOPHIA consistently preserves the traditional vocabulary that has shaped English-speaking Christianity. The most theologically significant divergence is PNEUMA's addition of "and sisters" in v.40 — a modern inclusive rendering that, while well-intentioned, resolves an interpretive ambiguity that scholarship has not settled.

---

*Generated by SOPHIA, AI Arbiter, Bible Translation Project*
